stem: By the Invertible Matrix Theorem, \( A \) is invertible if and only if \( \det(A) \ne 0 \). What is \( \det(A) \) for \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{pmatrix} \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses all plus signs instead of the alternating \( +,-,+ \) cofactor pattern.
This uses all minus signs after the first term instead of the alternating pattern.
This is the 'rule of Sarrus' diagonal-product sum, but with the second triple of diagonals omitted.
This is the trace (sum of diagonal entries), not the determinant.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: By the Invertible Matrix Theorem, an \( n\times n \) matrix is invertible if and only if its rank equals \( n \). What is the rank of \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{pmatrix} \), where row 3 is \( [[k1]] \) times row 1 plus \( [[k2]] \) times row 2?
solution: Since row 3 is a linear combination of rows 1 and 2, it contributes no new pivot; rows 1 and 2 remain independent since \( ([[a]])([[e]])-([[b]])([[d]]) \ne 0 \). The rank is [[answer]], which is less than \( n=3 \), so \( A \) is NOT invertible.
distractor_rationales: This treats all three rows as independent, ignoring the dependency in row 3.
A matrix with a nonzero row cannot have rank 0.
This under-counts the independent rows; both of the first two rows survive elimination.
The rank of a \(3\times3\) matrix can never exceed 3.
A rank cannot be negative.
The rank of a \(3\times3\) matrix can never exceed 3.

stem: By the Invertible Matrix Theorem, \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & k \end{pmatrix} \) fails to be invertible exactly when \( \det(A) = 0 \). For which value of \( k \) does this happen?
solution: \( A \) is singular exactly when \( \det(A) = [[a]]k - ([[b]])([[c]]) = 0 \), i.e. \( k = \dfrac{([[b]])([[c]])}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the product \( bc \); one must still divide by \( a \) to isolate \( k \).
This solves the determinant equation with the wrong sign.
This is the top-right entry, not the value of \( k \).
This is the bottom-left entry, not the value of \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: By the Invertible Matrix Theorem, \( A \) is invertible if and only if \( 0 \) is not an eigenvalue of \( A \), equivalently the product of its eigenvalues is nonzero. For the upper-triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{pmatrix} \), what is the product of its eigenvalues?
solution: The eigenvalues of a triangular matrix are its diagonal entries \( [[a]], [[d]], [[f]] \), all nonzero, so their product is \( ([[a]])([[d]])([[f]]) = [[answer]] \) (which equals \( \det(A) \), confirming \( A \) is invertible).
distractor_rationales: This sums the diagonal entries (the trace) instead of multiplying them.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies entries from the first row instead of the diagonal eigenvalues.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
